data.title: Silgan Holdings: The Free Cash Flow Is Accelerating
data.description:
Introduction

As the packaging and packaging-related business is coming out of a few tough quarters (and years, since COVID), some companies are still trading at interesting valuations. Silgan Holdings (NYSE:SLGN) is a stock I have been covering for almost five years now. The total return since that article was approximately 70%, which is fine, but really just in line with the performance of the broader index.

The focus remains on the free cash flow

Looking back at the first quarter of the year, the company posted a record performance, as its adjusted EBIT and adjusted EPS have never been higher. This confirmed the investment thesis I explained in my January article, as Silgan Holdings is on the right track. While the company still has to report on its Q2 results, having another look at the Q1 results and seeing how peers and competitors sound pretty upbeat about the second quarter could indicate Silgan Holdings’ cash flow performance could continue to improve from here on.

In that first quarter, the company reported a revenue of $1.47B, on which it generated a gross profit of $270L, which is an increase of in excess of 20% to the $223.5M in gross profit generated in Q1 2024. And although some of the other operating expenses also increased, the EBIT jumped by in excess of 15% to $130.5M.

Silgan saw a slightly higher net finance expense, but this increase wasn’t enough to derail the company’s improving performance. And with a pre-tax income of almost $88M and just under $68M on an after-tax basis, Silgan’s Q1 indeed was very robust. The net profit translated into an EPS of $0.64.

In all of my previous articles I have been focusing on Silgan’s free cash flow profile, as that’s ultimately what allows the company to repay its debt, buy back stock, and pay dividends. While the reported operating cash flow was pretty weak with a negative operating cash flow of $683M, investors need to keep in mind this is a seasonal issue and the very high investment in the working capital elements (for a total of almost $848M) will be converted back into cash throughout the year. This also means the net operating cash flow before changes in the working capital was approximately $164.2M.

The total capex was approximately $83M, resulting in a net free cash flow of approximately $81M. Divided over the current share count of roughly 107M shares, this represents a FCFPS of around $0.76.

Keep in mind the first quarter is relatively weak, and Silgan Holdings has confirmed it expects to generate an adjusted EPS of $4.00-4.20 in 2025, and the midpoint of that guidance represents a double-digit percentage increase over the $3.62 in adjusted EPS generated in 2024. The net free cash flow result will be around $450M, as Silgan expects a full-year capex of $300M. Given the $83M capex in Q1, this means the average quarterly capex in the next few quarters will be around $72M per quarter.

The $450M in full-year net free cash flow The strong cash flows also mean I’m giving the company’s bonds a good look, and the 4.125% February 2028 bond currently offers a yield to maturity of around 5.1% based on the midpoint. Although this represents a markup of just around 130 bps to the 3-year US Treasury yield, the strong cash flows generated by Silgan still make the 2028 bond an interesting investment opportunity for fixed-income investors.

Investment thesis

As Silgan Holdings is guiding for a full-year FCFPS of $4.20, the current share price of around $56 represents a free cash flow yield of approximately 7.5%, and that definitely means the stock isn’t expensive yet.

While I currently have no position in Silgan (as I was ‘hoping’ the share price would drop towards the lower $40 range during the post-Liberation Day uncertainty), I may write some (out of the money) put options on Silgan. The options are thinly traded, so if I’d put an order in the market, I’m entering the trade with the mindset to hold the written put option until the expiry date.

Silgan’s Q1 was strong, and I expect the Q2 results to confirm the strong performance and to validate the company’s earnings and free cash flow guidance.

data.title: Dell: An AI Investment For Value Investors
data.description:
Dell is a value investment, a growth investment, and a GARP investment. It's a bit of all 3, and that makes it an interesting investment

The market is said to be “frothy,” overvalued, and as speculative as the dot.com era at the turn of this century. I don’t want to try to enter that debate. About all I will say is that so far in 2025, appreciation percentages for major indices have been muted. The MAGS, for example, an ETF composed of the Magnificent 7 stocks said to be the focus of a bubble, has seen year-to-date appreciation of 6% (based on price as of 7/23). Bubbles usually see something a bit stronger than that. The IGV, which is the extended tech/software ETF, has had a year-to-date gain of around 10% again, not the stuff of bubbles.

That said, there is plenty of commentary from well-regarded strategists from BTIG and from a high-profile economist at Apollo who have made the case with regard to excess valuation and a bubble mentality. And there are plenty of tech stocks with bubble-like valuations that seem to attract enthusiasts almost regardless of their valuation. (I will leave my list of those stocks alone to avoid reader distraction). Some meme stocks, such as Opendoor, have seen outsized speculative moves.

In addition, there are other IT stocks that have seen substantial appreciation this year, but mainly because their results or their outlook have changed substantially. One of the differences between now and 25 years ago is that investors are paying for actual results; back then, so-called eyeballs and clicks sufficed.

This frothiness, so-called, has taken place against a backdrop of very rapid adoption of a revolutionary artificial intelligence paradigm, as yet underappreciated by some despite what might seem to others to be a naïve enthusiasm.

Is there a way for investors who are concerned about valuation but want to participate in some way in the AI revolution to find an appropriate investment vehicle? I believe that an investment in Dell may do the trick. No one will confuse Dell (NYSE:DELL) with a high-growth IT vendor. It is best known as a vendor of PCs, servers, and storage, and in recent years, those categories have hardly seen strong growth. Revenues last year were 6% lower than they had been in 2021, and profits have fallen as well. But for me, if not for a multi-year tale of woe, the shares would have a different valuation and would be far less investible.

Its current valuation metrics seem squarely in value territory. The company has an EV/S of about 1X and an enterprise value/EBITDA of 11X. Its forward P/E is less than 14X, and its Price/Earnings growth is all of 0.85X. And revenue growth last quarter, while certainly stronger than recent results, was all of 5%. It is a long way from being a high-growth company.

The shares have recently received some favorable analyst commentary, and they have risen by 7% over the last month and are up by 13% so far this year. But with the consensus earnings estimate showing earnings growth of 15%, valuations have remained anchored in place.

The real question at this point is whether Dell will be able to compound earnings growth at this rate or higher for the next several years. My belief is that the company can and will. It is mainly about the company’s AI servers, whose growth has become prodigious and is likely to remain so.

AI Servers: Dell’s Growth Engine

Dell is probably not the company or investment most associated with the AI revolution. After all, most of its revenue comes from the sale of PCs to consumers or enterprises-or is that the case? Last quarter, what Dell calls its infrastructure group had revenues of $10.3 billion, up by 12% year-over-year. Actually, server and networking revenues reached $6.3 billion, up 16%. That is the component of Dell selling AI infrastructure.

By now, the Client Solutions Group—the PC business—only accounts for 53% of revenues. It was able to grow by 5% overall, despite consumer PC revenues falling by 19% to $1.5 billion, or just 6% of Dell's total revenues. The Dell opportunity is not related to its consumer PC business at all, which continues to shrink. But it is first and foremost related to its AI infrastructure business, which had an extraordinary result last quarter, as yet not totally recognized in Dell’s share price and, as will be seen, not really totally accounted for in the company’s outlook. Here is the CEO's comment from the earnings conference call relating to Dell's AI server business.

Turning to AI. We experienced exceptionally strong demand for AI-optimized servers, building on the momentum discussed in February and further demonstrating that our differentiation is winning in the marketplace. We built (booked) $12.1 billion in orders in the first quarter, surpassing the entirety of shipments in all of FY 2025. We shipped $1.8 billion, leaving us with a backlog of $14.4 billion. Our five-quarter pipeline continued to grow sequentially across both Tier 2 CSPs and private and public enterprise customers and remains multiples of that backlog.

The company is actually anticipating that its AI server shipments will rise by 4X sequentially to $7 billion in Q2, or almost 25% of the company’s total Q2 revenue forecast of about $29 billion. Of course, that level of sequential growth is unsustainable. The company has provided what appears to be a hyper-conservative forecast for full-year AI server revenues.

We expect ISG to grow in the high-teens, driven by over $15 billion in AI server shipments and continued growth in traditional server and storage.

It is probably reasonable for a company such as Dell to err on the side of caution in its forecast, as it has been so long since it has been able to report sustainable growth. Overall, the company is projecting that 1H AI server shipments will be about $9 billion; the full-year forecast implies 2H server shipments of $6 billion despite both a record backlog and a rising pipeline. While I believe that it is more than likely for the company to exceed its ISG revenue and contribution margin targets this year simply because AI server revenue and bookings are showing such significant growth, doing so is not strictly necessary in order to recommend the shares.

That said, it is this hyper-conservative forecast that has constrained Dell’s share price and of course, bolstered the case to buy the shares. Dell shares were not up in the immediate wake of the announcement, although they are now up about 14% since the company last announced earnings. The next earnings date is scheduled for August 28; if there is another strong quarter of AI server growth, as I anticipate, I believe that this will almost certainly lead to a guidance increase and to a valuation rerating as additional analysts focus on the opportunity.

Not surprisingly, the company CEO was asked about the math and how the guide looked relative to internal expectations. The answers-and there were several questions and answers because the math is so striking- were long, and full of jargon and the issues of shipping everything that is being ordered-but the net of what was said is as follows:

And probably the interesting thing, what I think is the most interesting thing, is that we converted our five-quarter pipeline, obviously, to generate the $12.1 billion in the growing backlog, and the five-quarter pipeline grew. It grew sequentially. It's up significantly year-over-year. It's filled with lots of multiple types of technology, versions of Blackwell, and it now contains sovereign.

Also got lots to go. Three-quarters in front of us. I can really tell you we're on the plus side of $15 billion and feel pretty good about it. But we'll update you on how the full year goes in another quarter.

Obviously, we have a significant backlog already, and that will continue to expand. But at this juncture, we think we should remain on what we have put out for the guide and not do any dynamic (dramatic) increases there.

The specific numbers articulated are what they were and now are embedded in the consensus. But one further answer that I thought worth noting is this:

$15 billion plus with a capital E, capital L, capital U, capital S (the transcript should actually read capital P and not "E").

That is as far and as clear as the CEO was willing to go in suggesting that there was significant upside to the revenue growth forecast for AI servers.

I will put this another way—a hoary expression that has fallen out of favor. Dell has a tiger by the tail. Management is being prudent, as it should be in expressing the magnitude of the opportunity. It isn’t fully encapsulated in the guide, and it would not be reasonable if it were. It has created an unusually favorable set-up for investors wanting to get exposure to the AI opportunity but who are not willing to pay the valuation that is required to buy stock in almost any other AI-related company.

AI Servers: What is the market size, and what is Dell’s position in the market

As AI applications are now seeing broad adoption, a need has arisen for servers that are capable of running these applications at appropriate performance levels. AI servers specifically are optimized to run applications that are based on machine learning, including model training and deployment. Other functionality supported by AI Servers includes what is called deep learning, natural language processing, and neural network tasks. They are used for “big data” and analytic applications, as well as applications that provide intelligence to robots.

These servers are most often built around hardware based on Nvidia GPUs and often have Tensor Processing capabilities, which means they are optimized for parallel processing applications—a key requirement for both model training and reference processing. They often require what is called Fast Storage based on NVMe SSDs of the kind offered by Pure Storage (PSTG) and others.

Often, these servers come with specialized AI Frameworks preinstalled. These may include TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Scikit. To run properly, these servers often need liquid cooling and optimized networking services, such as the solutions being offered by Arista (ANET) and others. Dell's AI servers offer preinstalled TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Scikit capabilities, as well as liquid cooling.

The point to be made here is that this is a specialized category, and shouldn’t be confused as part of the overall server market. It is a truly substantial opportunity. As the link here shows, the market currently is estimated to be greater than $150 billion/year in revenue with a CAGR of greater than 34% through 2030.

If indeed, this projection is accurate-and I have no reason to believe that it isn’t-and Dell does nothing more than maintain its current market share, then AI server revenue for Dell would be equal to or greater than the current size of the company in terms of revenues by 2030. That is obviously quite a bit greater than current investor expectations.

Actually, at the moment, Dell has the leading position in the AI server market and is thought to be gaining share. While Supermicro (SMCI) has gotten a significant amount of investor attention and has indeed achieved substantial growth, Dell seems to have a market share of 7.2% and has overtaken Supermicro recently in terms of share, without getting much credit for the accomplishment. That 7.2% share was recorded before the extraordinary quarter that Dell recently reported in which bookings surged by almost 4X sequentially to a level greater than all of 2024.

Dell's AI Servers: Are they differentiated? Is there a competitive moat

For readers who don’t know me, I am a software analyst by background. I will say it in print—I hate like heck to analyze hardware companies. Moats and differentiation can vanish in weeks. I have often promised myself never to analyze a hardware company, although I have plenty of background in the storage and networking area, and I own shares of Pure Storage and Arista and have done so for many years.

Dell, as mentioned earlier, is gaining market share in the AI Server space despite numerous competitors. Dell maintains that its differentiators include purpose-designed servers-its PowerEdge XE 9680 and R760xa, which have high-performance compute capabilities that some other competitive offerings currently do not match. Dell has also evolved partnerships with a variety of GPU sources, primarily Nvidia (NVDA), of course, but also AMD (AMD) and Intel (INTC). This allows users to pick from a menu of architectures and performance capabilities rather than being limited to the kind of compute resources they can procure.

Many readers—and honestly, this writer—have to chuckle when seeing cooling listed as a differentiator. How high-tech can cooling be?

But the reality is that these days, AI datacenters generate a massive amount of heat. They have to be cooled. And Dell claims that both its air-cooled and liquid-cooled technologies do the job efficiently. It offers something called a PowerCool real-door heat exchanger, which, it claims, improves the power consumption necessary to dissipate heat and manages thermal demands. The question evaluating these types of claims is always how Dell’s technology compares to alternatives and whether, or more likely when, competitors leapfrog what is a current advantage.

Dell claims that it has management tools such as what is called IDRAC, OpenManage Enterprise, and CloudIQ. These are all observability and remediation tools—the equivalent in hardware for what Datadog (DDOG) and Dynatrace (DT) offer for software applications.

The company’s AI servers incorporate some security features, and in particular, data protection for AI. There is technology now to help validate some of these claims. A company called Principled Technologies does offer 3rd party validation with regard to just how differentiated Dell’s offerings are these days. I have linked here to their study, which focuses on comparing Dell with SuperMicro. The report speaks for itself, and indeed, Dell’s management and security capabilities are simply superior to what SuperMicro actually offers.

Another source validates claims regarding Dell’s capabilities when it comes to cooling and power consumption.

This focus on energy efficiency is critical in the AI era, where data center energy consumption is a growing concern. Competitors also offer cooling solutions, but Dell's specific innovations, like the eRDHx and its integration into their rack solutions, provide a competitive edge in power efficiency and capacity utilization.

Cooling and power consumption really are key differentiators in the age of immense AI data centers. Pure Storage has partially built its company because it offers power and space advantages. And it has maintained those advantages for several years at this point. I have linked here to a research report that lists the major competitors and also identifies key competitive factors, which do indeed include cooling and the rack form factor, as possible reasons why Dell has been growing its market share and suggests why it may continue to be able to do so.

Dell’s other businesses: Evaluating their potential

Dell is expected to reach revenues of just greater than $100 billion this year. While I think its current forecast of $15 billion in AI server revenue is exceptionally constrained, even if results are 30%–50% greater than that particular forecast, AI servers will still only represent 20%+ of Dell’s business. Much as I would like to avoid writing about these other areas, they still are really the dog—AI servers are still just a tail—a key tail but not all-encompassing in an analysis nonetheless.

At a high level, the math is pretty straightforward. If the 20% of Dell’s revenue that is AI servers grows at a market rate, i.e., around 35%, and the 80% of Dell’s business that can be thought of as legacy grows at 5%, that yields a CAGR of 11%. Currently, the revenue growth estimate for this year is about 8%, and for the next fiscal year, the consensus revenue growth estimate is about 7%. Those are, self-evidently, quite undemanding estimates.

There are several specific product categories that Dell calls out in its earnings presentation. Probably the traditional server segment is the product category with the most potential. There is not a huge amount of net new demand for traditional servers. Revenue growth in this segment is a function of customers consolidating and modernizing their data centers. Dell is now selling what it labels as 16th- and 17th-generation servers, while a substantial proportion of its server base is still using 14th-generation. Dell’s latest server generation was released earlier this year and has some of the same benefits to users as its AI servers. These benefits include significantly enhanced performance for many workloads, enhanced energy efficiency, and built-in security and reliability capabilities.

Dell’s traditional server business and networking business had revenues of around $4.5 billion. Traditional servers were about 19% of revenue last quarter, with growth moderating to high single digits compared to low double digits the prior quarter.

Dell is probably the market share leader in the enterprise storage space, with a share in the high 20% range that has remained fairly consistent since it purchased EMC almost 10 years ago. It has a very broad portfolio, with quite a bit of overlap. Its high-end product is derived from EMC technology, and its object storage is a descendant of Isilon, another part of what came to the company with the EMC merger.

While Dell has bits and pieces of storage technology that are market leading, it is probably losing share to Pure overall; users are migrating steadily to all-flash, a technology that Pure leads, and that trend, which is years old at this point, hasn’t abated.

Dell, as an incumbent vendor in many cases, has market advantages of scale and product breadth, but disadvantages since not all of its storage products run on the same platform. Dell has an offering called PowerStore. PowerStore is a storage backup technology that competes against a variety of offerings, including Rubrik (RBRK) and Pure. It is growing in double digits, but at far lower percentage rates than the above-named competitors.

Overall, last quarter, Dell’s storage portfolio enjoyed its third quarter of growth, with revenues increasing by 6%. Storage is a highly cyclical business, and unless a competitor sits athwart a major industry trend such as the migration to all-flash, growth is unlikely to exceed the mid-single digits and can show perturbations around that kind of central tendency rate. Storage revenues these days are about 16% of the company’s total.

Dell is a major competitor in the PC market. In Q1 2025, Dell’s PC market share was 16.3%, in 3rd place overall and down slightly from 16.7% in the year-earlier period. The top 2 vendors in the PC space are Lenovo and HP (HPQ). I have linked here to a 3rd party analysis that shows Dell’s market share over the past 14 years. The company’s share has risen modestly over that time.

The 3rd party analysis linked here shows that the PC market is actually showing some growth. That said, the market is very cyclical and has down years as well as growth years.

Recently, Dell’s CEO, Jeff Clarke, named himself as head of the Client Solutions Group, i.e., the PC operation. The former head of CSG is now head of corporate strategy. This reshuffle seems an acknowledgement that Dell’s senior management believes it is not achieving corporate objectives. Just how much of that is market-related and how much is related to possible Dell marketing missteps is impossible to determine. The PC business has been tough for Dell—and for its competitors—for years. Overall, CSG quarterly revenues had been over $15 billion in the middle of 2022 and were $12.5 billion last quarter. Consumer revenue was $1.5 billion, down 19% year on year, a worse result than the 12% decrease in the past quarter. Commercial revenue, however, rose by 9% last quarter, accelerating from 5% in the prior quarter. Commercial revenues are now 88% of CSG revenues, so overall, CSG revenues rose by 5% last quarter compared to 1% in the prior sequential quarter.

The company is forecasting that CSG revenues will climb in the low-mid single digits in Q2. That would be a small sequential revenue increase and consistent with Q1 revenue growth performance. In thinking about Dell’s prospective growth outside of AI servers, I have considered that a 5% CAGR is realistic and conservative. The upside, if any, is going to come if Dell can improve its attach rate to those servers—all of its servers in fact.

And if I extrapolate that to what can be done in an enterprise, it's the same sort of opportunity. In fact, we think the opportunity to attach storage, particularly as the data structure moves to more object storage and our assets in the unstructured space, we have a differentiated advantage. We're going to continue to invest in that portfolio. The opportunity in networking is there. And obviously, the services that I just mentioned are equally important in enterprises. They are in the largest clusters in the world. So we remain optimistic. We have work to do. I would not be truthful if I said we were satisfied. We are not satisfied with the attachment today. (attach rate) I think that is all upside and opportunity. Our sales team is focused on that. And we continue to find opportunities to differentiate our portfolio, look at some of the subsystems in our performance and the attributes that it brings to what's being done in these modern workloads, and we're very optimistic about that.

Driving the attach rate could be significant to growth, but it is very much a work in progress. To the extent that it happens, it would be a lagniappe to my own expectation for Dell to achieve low double-digit revenue growth over the next several years. The components of the attached opportunity are all very competitive markets with best-of-breed competitors.

Dell’s Business Model: Lots of moving parts to consider

Dell, in some ways, is a complicated company in terms of the many different buckets from which it earns revenue and cash flow. The company reports some but not all KPIs on a segment basis. The company divested VMware a few years ago. But it still has some revenues from VMware Resale, which are continuing to decline. This distorts the actual growth the company is achieving.

Last quarter, reportable segment revenues rose by almost 8%. The company has guided revenue growth of 16% in Q2, reflecting the spike in expected AI server revenues, and revenue growth of 8% for the full year. As mentioned, I have modelled for 11% growth this year, as I expect the revenues from AI servers will noticeably exceed the $15 billion target.

Dell is a hardware company, and its cost ratios are very different than those of the software companies I usually write about and analyze. It doesn’t have a significant level of SBC since its employee count is much lower than would be the case for a typical software company of similar size.

Overall, last quarter saw margins improve rather significantly. Operating margins rose 70 basis points to 5%. That took place despite a 70 basis point decline in gross margins. Gross margins were 21% of revenues last quarter. The gross margin decline was essentially a function of mix swings in terms of geography. The SG&A expense ratio fell from 14.1% to 12.6%, while the R&D expense ratio rose from 3.4% to 3.5%. The company accrued an 11% tax rate this year compared to a significant tax credit in the year-earlier period. The company is continuing to tightly manage SG&A expenses; overall, it is expecting operating margins to contract this quarter and to grow for the full year.

The consensus EPS forecast for this current fiscal year is now about $9.40; the company raised this forecast by 2%+ when it last reported earnings. I expect further increases in the earnings forecast as the year progresses, but given the 21% gross margin level, the operating leverage in this model is nothing like what is the case for software companies.

Dell achieved a very strong operating cash flow margin last quarter. Much of this was balance sheet-related. In particular, receivables fell and payables rose. Some of that was related to seasonal factors. Over the course of a full year, the company’s free cash flow margin and its operating margin should be converging.

Dell’s valuation is low for a growth company and reasonable for a GARP company.

When many analysts, including myself, look at software companies using a SaaS revenue model, it is typical to look at a Rule of 40 metric. The Rule of 40 is composed of the estimated CAGR and the free cash flow margin. At least that is how I do it. Dell has nothing close to a rule of 40 business model, and I doubt if it ever will. The margins on hardware, no matter what, are simply too low, and Dell will always have a huge amount of revenue coming from low-growth areas of the IT space, such as PCs and enterprise storage.

The other side of that coin is that the company’s EV/S ratio is low. Dell has a current enterprise value of around $106 billion. My revenue estimate for the next 4 quarters is $108 billion. So the EV/S is less than 1X.

In a company like this, the P/EG ratio is important. Dell, based on my expectation for EPS of $9.60, has a P/E ratio of about 13X. Earnings growth is projected to be 16%-17% over the next year. So, the PEG ratio is just greater than 8X. That is deep into value territory.

Price targets from brokerage analysts haven’t moved up with the company’s operational performance. That is mainly because the company chose to leave its revenue target unchanged despite the exceptional AI server booking and backlog. An increase in revenue guidance—particularly an increase in the guidance for AI server revenue—is likely to see multiple price target changes and notable share price appreciation for Dell.

Risks to the investment thesis

The major part of the Dell investment thesis relates to the growth of its AI server business. My belief is that this business will, at the very least, achieve the growth of 34%+ forecast for AI servers. I actually expect that the CAGR for that business will exceed the overall growth in the market based on the many functional advantages offered by Dell's AI servers. If, for some unforeseen reason, Dell is unable to execute and capture the market opportunity, the investment thesis will be vitiated.

Another risk to the thesis will also be what Dell guides in terms of growth. It chose to be very cautious with its guidance despite the strong AI booking attainment last quarter. It could continue to choose conservatism as a de-risking mechanism in what remains an unsettled macro environment.

Much of the rest of what Dell sells is cyclically sensitive. There are certainly many risks to global growth expectations. From tariffs to interest rates to political dysfunction—the list goes on and on. Were there to be a recession, the non-AI components of Dell’s business would suffer to a greater or lesser extent.

While I call the attached rate opportunity one of lagniappe, part of the investment thesis is for Dell to do a better job in selling storage and networking products to its server customers. It will not be an easy undertaking.

Finally, the fact that the company’s CEO chose to appoint himself to run the CSG component of Dell’s business is not likely to be a great sign with regard to sales of PCs this quarter or the outlook for that business in the immediate future. I think it is fair to say that expectations for Dell’s PC business are fairly low, but there is always something below “fairly low.”

I should reemphasize the point that Dell is not now and is unlikely to be a company achieving hypergrowth. While it is difficult to be precise with regard to how investors perceive Dell and their view on appropriate share valuation metrics, I doubt that many growth stock investors actually own Dell shares, but I doubt as well that a multi-year CAGR beyond the low double digits is possible in the foreseeable future. As a current shareholder, I would be delighted if that were to happen, but raising expectations beyond reasonable levels is something I try to avoid at all costs.

Wrapping Up: The case to buy Dell shares now

About 2 months ago, Dell reported an exceptional quarter for AI Server bookings. A bit of that upside showed up in Q1 results; some more of it is part of the company’s Q2 forecast. And some of it resides in a level of backlog for AI servers substantially greater than the full-year results of revenue from that category than had been achieved in all of FY ’25. The company chose not to embed the strong backlog growth in AI servers as part of its full-year forecast in terms of revenue growth.

AI servers are a relatively new category in the market. They have ultra-high-performance computing capabilities, a requisite for genAI referent processing. Dell’s AI servers have advantages in terms of their ability to offer a holistic and integrated solution for running all kinds of AI workloads. The AI servers that Dell sells are based on the latest iterations of Nvidia and AMD GPUs. Dell’s servers have liquid cooling capabilities, another key requisite for high-performance computing. The company has some unique deployment options (what they call IRSS). Its servers are integrated with some of its storage solutions. It apparently offers energy-efficient designs that give it total cost-of-ownership advantages.

Depending on the exact trajectory of AI Server growth at Dell, that component of TH1 will reach 20% or more as a share of company revenues with an expected CAGR of 35%—the estimated growth of that market segment. That CAGR is more likely than not to exceed even that pace if Dell continues to gain market share, as I believe is the most likely scenario.

The balance of Dell’s revenues comes from a fairly traditional mix of servers, storage, networking switches, and PCs. Some of these have strong and growing market shares; others are less competitive. On balance, these products are maintaining market share, although the traditional server segment is apparently achieving above market growth rates.

At this point, the company’s attach rate, i.e., the attach of storage and networking products to its servers, remains disappointing. The company is focused on improving the attach rate, but my estimates are based on nothing more than market growth rates for those segments of its business. Overall, my model is based on Dell achieving 35% growth for 20% of its business and 5% growth for the other 80%.

Overall, I believe that Dell can achieve a low double-digit CAGR for the next several years. This should allow some increase in operating margins, although the margins in any given period will also be influenced significantly by both product and geographic revenue mix.

That provides Dell shares with attractive valuation metrics. These include an EV/S of less than 1X, a P/EG ratio of about 0.81.3X, and a P/E of about 13X.

While Dell shares have rallied this year, their 15% Y-T-D appreciation is far from reflecting the company’s outlook and opportunities. I believe that the shares are a reasonable choice for investors looking to invest in the AI boom without paying what some consider to be astronomical valuations.

I have bought the shares in one of my portfolios and recommend them to investors looking for positive alpha with less risk than a typical high-growth investment.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics Q2: Confidently Continuing The Recovery
data.description:
Viking Therapeutics Stock: Outperformance Is Possible

Biotech companies (XBI) are starting to make a comeback of sorts, as XBI has even managed to outperform the broader healthcare peers (XLV) since the April lows. However, that shouldn't have been considered as an accolade since XLV has been one of the weaker/lagging performers among the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) sector plays in 2025.

As a result, while the stock of Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VKTX) is still down more than 30% over the past one year, my decision to upgrade the stock since March 2025, appears to have paid off. Accordingly, VKTX has outperformed the SPX since then, suggesting that profitable setups on early-stage companies like Viking Therapeutics are workable with the correct setups.

In my April VKTX writeup, I made sure to remind investors that the pre-revenue stages mean that taking a longer term approach is necessary in an otherwise monolithic obesity drugs market. Hence, paying close attention toward VKTX's commercialization path is pivotal as it helps validate the robustness of its technology vis-à-vis its well-established GLP-1 peers in tackling obesity and diabetes care.

Hence, no surprises for me that VKTX hasn't collapsed much further after the initial post-earnings decline as Viking Therapeutics delivered its Q2 earnings release. I believe the market is right to ignore the worries of the near-term earnings miss imputed to higher R&D expenses, as investors refocused on the progress made in its pipeline for VK2735.

VKTX: Pipeline Progress Assures Investors Of Better Times Ahead

As a reminder, VKTX's dual track GLP-1 approach (injectables and oral) to take on the market leaders right now in Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) and Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) has attained notable milestones. Hence, it should position the company favorably, as the VK2735 subcutaneous therapy enters Phase III studies on obesity and type-2 diabetes indications. In addition, its oral formulation has also entered Phase II trials. And it marks a solid step after its Phase I multiple ascending dose study that delivered 8.2% in weight loss after 28 days, aligning itself as a possible best-in-class product compared to the current candidates by LLY and NVO.

While a longer duration study at the next phase is obviously required, the company indicated that top-line data could be ready in the second half of this year, underscoring the confidence in its study performance. Hence, I believe investors will pay close attention to the results at the 13-week mark, and if expectations for "efficacy and tolerability" are in line, we might even see the company accelerate the progress toward Phase III studies (and thus making a bolder step toward commercialization).

Therefore, I believe we can reasonably appraise that Viking Therapeutics is making a firm commitment to deliver best-in-class therapies, paving the way for subsequent monetization or even spur a possible full-fledged acquisition by the leading biopharma peers keen on subsuming its high-potential tech.

As a result, I believe the rapid developments are helping to shape the obesity drugs race that has taken the US by storm, as LLY closed the gap on Novo's previous leadership.

Could the $3.8B market cap VKTX be an attractive play for biopharma leaders looking to capture the underlying economics of what seems to be a rather broad pipeline assets (add in pipeline assets picture). The scale and growth of the obesity drugs market over the next five to ten years should benefit players/upstarts who are inching nearer or close to commercializing their technology.

Yet, we must also be cognizant that The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) research lowered their obesity drug TAM projections by 25% recently. Hence, we cannot simply assume such forecasts are cast in stone, while trying to triangulate a rapidly evolving market that could also stymie first movers like NVO, as LLY demonstrated.

However, with the lucrative market still expected to be considered as one of the structurally growing opportunities in the battered healthcare space right now (facing political, regulatory, and industry pricing headwinds), I believe VKTX's candidates will be closely watched by the market as other biopharma leaders look to reignite the bullish recovery.

VKTX: Cash Runway A Concern, But Not Immediate

Hence, making sure VKTX has a long enough cash runway to tank the R&D costs to reach the commercialization stage is crucial. Based on the $808M in liquidity position that the company posted in Q2, it appears to be enough to absorb cash burn for some time in FY2027 before additional financing could be necessary.

Hence, the relatively long pathway for VKTX to validate the results from its ongoing VK2735 clinical studies should help alleviate downward pressure driven by short sellers (VKTX's short interest ratio surpassed 26% at the end of June). These pessimists are arguably betting that the company's progress could face insurmountable stumbles along the way that make maintaining its current $3.8B market valuation unsustainable.

Underlying risks to the company's progress in the otherwise potentially significant segment could stymie its path toward commercialization. I do not think that the manufacturing capacity limitation is too much of an issue now, hobbling investor sentiments. Proving the tech is arguably the first order problem of greater magnitude that must be resolved with efficiency.

Buy Or Sell VKTX Stock?

Hence, with top-line data and other results expected over the next 12 months, I believe we should have a better idea of whether they could compel these bearish investors to consider covering their positions earlier than later. The continued advance in VKTX from its April lows suggests the stock arguably reached steep oversold status then, augmenting the upward momentum recovery as dip buyers reached in to help support the stock from falling further into the trenches. Also, I assess that VKTX's cash runway looks relatively reasonable to secure the company's pathway through 2027, while it primes for key data releases in the next 12 months.

With the stock still maintaining its recovery from the April lows as I indicated in my previous article, I urge investors who bought those lows to maintain their posture.

New investors contemplating entering should ruminate about their capital allocation, given its early-stage nature and unprofitability through the 2029 forecast period. Investors should judiciously regard the opportunity in VKTX as highly speculative, and I don't encourage allocating VKTX as one of the core positions in your portfolio.

Rating: Maintain Speculative Buy.

data.title: Almirall, S.A. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Almirall, S.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Intel Q2: Learning The Hard Way
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Intel Corporation’s (INTC) second quarter results highlight a company in transition that still has to digest some painful lessons.

The company is still hemorrhaging money, and the CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, is attempting to carry out a large restructuring.

There are some glimmers of hope for Intel, but nothing really to be excited about at the moment.

I still rate Intel a Buy, but this one could take years to pay off.

A Quarter of Contradictions

It's been a pretty rough quarter for Intel, but I stand by my last Buy rating, as I believe Intel is indeed tackling the most important issues it faces.

I mentioned in my last article specifically the importance of increasing profit margins, providing clear Foundry guidance, and also gaining market share in AI.

Let’s see how Intel did in these key areas and the quarter overall.

Intel’s Q2 results were mixed at best. Revenue came in at $12.9 billion, just above guidance and showing slight improvement YoY.

But Intel also posted an adjusted loss of $0.10 per share, well below Wall Street's expectations. The company lost over $2.9 billion.

The reason behind this was large restructuring charges, totaling around $800 million in impairments and $200 million in one-time/costs

Excluding these charges, our second-quarter non-GAAP gross margin would have been 37.5% and non-GAAP EPS would have been $0.10, both results ahead of our Q2 guidance.

Source: Earnings Call.

Overall, revenue is better than expected, but earnings are much worse. Let’s break this down into segments now.

PC revenues came in at $7.9 billion, a 3% YoY decrease, but the company expects AI PCs to spark a new upgrade cycle.

Data center and AI, which should be the growth engine of the company, increased a meager 4%. But at least, Lip-Buu Tan acknowledges the problems in the earnings call.

In the past, we approached AI with a traditional silicon and training-centric mindset without a cohesive silicon systems software stack and strategy.

Source: Earnings Call.

This is a major gap Intel intends to close.

Foundry revenue came in at $4.4 billion, up slightly from last year but down QoQ.

18A reached a key milestone with the start of production wafers in Arizona ahead of Intel Products Q4 launch of its next-generation client product code named Panther Lake.

Source: Earnings Call.

But the company is clearly lagging in this area too.

A Hard But Necessary Lesson

But the most important development was perhaps the big restructuring Intel has undergone. Management has already been cut by 50% and Intel looks to be on track to reduce its workforce by 75,000 employees in 2025.

In terms of Foundry, Intel is also looking at consolidating and leaning out.

Unfortunately, the capacity investment we made over the last several years were well ahead of demand and were unwise and excessive. Our factory footprint has become needlessly fragmented.

Source: Earnings Call.

It seems like Intel got ahead of itself with Foundry, but hopefully this lesson has been learned and more caution will be applied moving forward.

For now, projects in Germany and Poland have been scrapped, and Costa Rica operations are being consolidated into Asia.

CapEx is now capped at $18 billion for 2025 and will likely be even smaller next year.

What Comes Next?

The biggest milestone for Intel moving forward will be the release of Panther Lake

Our top priority is delivering our first Panther Lake SKU by year-end.

Source: Earnings Call.

This will be key if Intel wants to stay relevant in the hardware game. And as far as AI is concerned, this is what Tan had to say

While we do need to build and consolidate upon our silicon franchise based upon our x86 CPU and our Xe GPUs, we recognize the need to move up the abstraction stack into system and software,

Source: Earnings Call.

Intel is trying to carve itself a niche in the AI market by using a more integral approach. However, it's going to be very hard for Intel at this point to catch up with Nvidia (NVDA) and other competitors in the space.

A Long Road to Profitability

Revenue next quarter will decrease, and while margins will improve, the company should just about break even.

In the earnings call, CFO David Zinsner mentioned margins would continue to be under pressure in 2025 and might improve in 2026 after Panther Lake has been ramped up.

My 2 Cents

Intel has made some costly mistakes, and the market is rightfully punishing the company.

Nonetheless, progress is being made, albeit slowly, in the key areas I highlighted. Intel is now going to revolutionize the AI space any time soon, but it can still regain profitability and be a worthwhile investment.

With new product releases and a possible PC upgrade cycle coming in next year, it’s possible we could see some traction in 2026.

I still think Intel is a Buy at today’s price and cheap valuation.

With a price/book value of 1 and price/cash flow under 10, it's definitely an enticing buy.

If Intel can return to a degree of normalcy and implement its cost-cutting plans, then this will once again make money, and investors will no longer be able to ignore this opportunity.

For this reason, I believe Intel has a place in a diversified portfolio.
